title: How is the performance of TreeSet compared to HashSet, and when would you choose one over the other?

answers.junior: HashSet offers O(1) average-case time complexity for add, remove, and contains operations, backed by a HashMap with hash-based bucket storage, making it significantly faster for most use cases. TreeSet, backed by a Red-Black Tree, provides O(log n) time complexity for the same operations, roughly 2-3x slower for large datasets.
answers.middle: HashSet offers O(1) average-case time complexity for add, remove, and contains operations, backed by a HashMap with hash-based bucket storage, making it significantly faster for most use cases. TreeSet, backed by a Red-Black Tree, provides O(log n) time complexity for the same operations, roughly 2-3x slower for large datasets. The fundamental trade-off is speed versus ordering: TreeSet maintains elements in their natural sorted order or by a provided Comparator, while HashSet provides no ordering guarantees whatsoever. Choose HashSet when you need fast membership checks, deduplication, or set operations (union, intersection) without caring about element order, for example tracking visited URLs in a web crawler or caching unique session IDs. Choose TreeSet when you require sorted iteration, range queries, or need to access floor/ceiling/higher/lower elements using the NavigableSet API, for example maintaining a leaderboard, implementing a scheduling system, or finding the nearest element to a given value.
answers.senior: HashSet offers O(1) average-case time complexity for add, remove, and contains operations, backed by a HashMap with hash-based bucket storage, making it significantly faster for most use cases. TreeSet, backed by a Red-Black Tree, provides O(log n) time complexity for the same operations, roughly 2-3x slower for large datasets. The fundamental trade-off is speed versus ordering: TreeSet maintains elements in their natural sorted order or by a provided Comparator, while HashSet provides no ordering guarantees whatsoever. Choose HashSet when you need fast membership checks, deduplication, or set operations (union, intersection) without caring about element order, for example tracking visited URLs in a web crawler or caching unique session IDs. Choose TreeSet when you require sorted iteration, range queries, or need to access floor/ceiling/higher/lower elements using the NavigableSet API, for example maintaining a leaderboard, implementing a scheduling system, or finding the nearest element to a given value. TreeSet implements NavigableSet and SortedSet interfaces, giving you powerful methods like subSet(from, to), headSet(to), tailSet(from), first(), last(), floor(), ceiling(), and pollFirst()/pollLast() that are simply unavailable in HashSet. In production scenarios, TreeSet shines when you need time-window sliding operations or ordered processing queues. One critical consideration: TreeSet requires elements to implement Comparable or you must provide a Comparator at construction time; failing to do so results in a ClassCastException at runtime. Memory footprint also differs: each TreeMap.Entry (backing TreeSet) stores left, right, and parent node references plus a color flag, consuming more memory than a HashSet bucket entry.
answers.tricky: HashSet offers O(1) average-case time complexity for add, remove, and contains operations, backed by a HashMap with hash-based bucket storage, making it significantly faster for most use cases. TreeSet, backed by a Red-Black Tree, provides O(log n) time complexity for the same operations, roughly 2-3x slower for large datasets. The fundamental trade-off is speed versus ordering: TreeSet maintains elements in their natural sorted order or by a provided Comparator, while HashSet provides no ordering guarantees whatsoever. Choose HashSet when you need fast membership checks, deduplication, or set operations (union, intersection) without caring about element order, for example tracking visited URLs in a web crawler or caching unique session IDs. Choose TreeSet when you require sorted iteration, range queries, or need to access floor/ceiling/higher/lower elements using the NavigableSet API, for example maintaining a leaderboard, implementing a scheduling system, or finding the nearest element to a given value. TreeSet implements NavigableSet and SortedSet interfaces, giving you powerful methods like subSet(from, to), headSet(to), tailSet(from), first(), last(), floor(), ceiling(), and pollFirst()/pollLast() that are simply unavailable in HashSet. In production scenarios, TreeSet shines when you need time-window sliding operations or ordered processing queues. One critical consideration: TreeSet requires elements to implement Comparable or you must provide a Comparator at construction time; failing to do so results in a ClassCastException at runtime. Memory footprint also differs: each TreeMap.Entry (backing TreeSet) stores left, right, and parent node references plus a color flag, consuming more memory than a HashSet bucket entry. For thread-safe sorted sets, consider wrapping TreeSet with Collections.synchronizedSortedSet() or using ConcurrentSkipListSet, which provides thread-safe O(log n) operations with similar ordering guarantees. A practical example: new TreeSet<>(Arrays.asList(5, 2, 8, 1, 9)).first() returns 1 immediately, whereas with HashSet you would need to iterate and track the minimum yourself.
